does: This paper investigates steering language model refusal at inference time by amplifying sparse autoencoder (SAE) features that mediate refusal behavior, rather than modifying weights through retraining. The authors implement activation steering to strengthen refusal-related features and evaluate its effectiveness against single-turn and multi-turn jailbreak attempts. They demonstrate that this steering approach successfully improves robustness against adversarial prompts, but critically discover a fundamental trade-off: amplifying refusal features systematically degrades model performance across multiple benchmark tasks, including on safe inputs unrelated to refusal. This performance degradation reveals unexpected entanglement between safety-relevant features and general language capabilities, suggesting that refusal mechanisms are more deeply integrated into core model functionality than previously assumed.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability, particularly the use of sparse autoencoders to identify and manipulate interpretable features in language models. It extends prior research on activation steering and inference-time safety interventions that aim to avoid the computational and data costs of retraining. The paper responds to growing interest in understanding how language models implement refusal and safety behaviors, building on work examining the internal representations that govern model outputs. It also connects to broader research on the trade-offs between safety and capability in language models, contributing empirical evidence to longstanding questions about whether safety and performance are necessarily in tension or can be cleanly decoupled through precise feature manipulation.
enables: This work opens important questions for future research on the nature of safety features in language models and whether refusal can be truly isolated from general capabilities. It motivates investigation into whether other safety interventions suffer similar hidden performance costs that may go undetected without comprehensive evaluation. The findings suggest new directions for designing safety mechanisms that avoid entanglement with core language understanding—whether through better feature decomposition methods, alternative architectural approaches, or hybrid strategies combining multiple safety mechanisms. This research also points toward developing more sophisticated evaluation protocols that detect subtle capability degradation, and encourages exploration of whether certain safety objectives are fundamentally incompatible with maintaining full model performance, with implications for how we approach responsible deployment of powerful language models.
